title: flipud
summary: Flip an array up-to-down along the first dimension.

gpu_support.notes: Uses the generic flip provider hook with axis=0; falls back to gatherâ†’flipâ†’upload when unavailable.

description: `flipud(A)` mirrors `A` across its horizontal axis, reversing the order of rows (dimension 1). It works with scalars, vectors, matrices, N-D tensors, logical arrays, character arrays, string arrays, cell arrays, complex data, and gpuArray handles, matching MATLAB semantics.
behaviors: Always reverses dimension 1 (rows) and leaves all other dimensions untouched, even for rank > 2 data.
Inputs with a single row (row vectors, scalars) are returned unchanged because the first dimension is singleton.
Numeric, logical, complex, character, string, and cell arrays all retain their MATLAB types, layout, and metadata (including UTF-16 code units for char arrays).

gpuArray inputs execute on the device via the generic `flip` provider hook (axis = 0); when that hook is unavailable, RunMat gathers once, mirrors the data on the host, and uploads the result so the returned value is still a gpuArray.
Dimensions larger than `ndims(A)` are treated as singleton axes, so `flipud` never errors when `A` has rank < 1.
Behaviour matches `flip(A, 1)` exactly; `flipud` is provided for readability and compatibility with existing MATLAB code.

examples.description: Flip the First Dimension of a 3-D Tensor
Flip Characters in a Char Array Vertically
Keep gpuArray Results on the Device While Flipping Rows

faqs.question: Does `flipud` change row vectors?
faqs.answer: No. A row vector has a singleton first dimension, so reversing that axis leaves the data unchanged.

faqs.question: Is `flipud` the same as calling `flip(A, 1)`?
faqs.answer: Yes. `flipud` is a convenience wrapper around `flip` that always targets dimension 1 (rows).

faqs.question: Can I apply `flipud` to N-D tensors?
faqs.answer: Absolutely. Only dimension 1 is reversed; all other axes keep their original order regardless of rank.

faqs.question: Does `flipud` support string, character, and cell arrays?
faqs.answer: Yes. String arrays reorder their elements, character arrays mirror each column while preserving UTF-8 data, and cell arrays reverse their rows without copying contained values.

faqs.question: What happens on the GPU if there is no flip kernel?
faqs.answer: RunMat gathers the tensor once, mirrors it on the CPU, and uploads the result so you still receive a gpuArray.

faqs.question: Does `flipud` allocate new GPU buffers?
faqs.answer: Providers may reuse storage, but the builtin always returns a fresh handle. The simple provider uploads a new buffer.

faqs.question: Is `flipud` numerically stable?
faqs.answer: Yes. The function only reorders elements; values are never modified, so it is numerically stable.

faqs.question: What does `flipud` do in MATLAB?
faqs.answer: â€” `B = flipud(A)` returns `A` with the order of its rows reversed, so `B(i, :) = A(end - i + 1, :)`. The number of columns and every higher-dimension page stays exactly the same.

faqs.question: What's the difference between `flipud`, `fliplr`, and `flip`?
faqs.answer: â€” `flipud` reverses dimension 1 (rows), `fliplr` reverses dimension 2 (columns), and `flip(A, dim)` reverses any dimension you pick. Modern MATLAB code tends to use `flip(A, dim)` directly; `flipud` and `fliplr` are convenience wrappers kept for readability.

faqs.question: How do I reverse a column vector?
faqs.answer: â€” For a column vector, `flipud(v)` returns the reversed vector. A dimension-agnostic idiom that works for row or column vectors is `v(end:-1:1)`.

gpu_residency: You typically do not need to call `gpuArray` directly. RunMatâ€™s auto-offload planner keeps tensors on the GPU when profitable and only gathers when a provider lacks the flip hook. Even in that fallback, `flipud` uploads the flipped result back to the device so subsequent operations remain gpu-resident.
gpu_behavior: RunMat first tries to execute `flipud` on the GPU by delegating to the providerâ€™s generic `flip` implementation with axis `0` (zero-based). If the provider does not implement this hook, RunMat transparently gathers the tensor, performs the vertical flip on the host, and uploads the result back to the device so residency is preserved.

points: `A` is a scalar, vector, matrix, or N-D array. It may hold any MATLAB class: numeric, logical, complex, character, string, or cell. `gpuArray` inputs are supported and stay device-resident when the provider offers a `flip` hook.

`B` is the same size and class as `A` with its first dimension reversed: `B(i, :, ...) = A(end - i + 1, :, ...)`. All other dimensions are preserved. For arrays with more than two dimensions, higher pages are treated independently.
`flipud(A)` is equivalent to `flip(A, 1)`. Use `fliplr(A)` to reverse columns instead, or `flip(A, dim)` when you need a dimension other than 1 or 2.
